comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's almost midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The picture?  I'm working my way through photos of my lineage, focusing on the teachers whose work has influenced me and the teachers who influenced them in turn.  Like Allan Bennett and George Cecil Jones, who were introduced over the last two weeks, this week's honoree was a teacher of Aleister Crowley. Theodor Reuss was a German occultist and Freemason who, like our past honoree Sylvester Gould, was a member of the Hermetic Brotherhood of Light, the successor order of the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor (HB of L). He founded and headed the Ordo Templi Orientis (OTO), a magical lodge originally envisioned by Austrian occultist Carl Kellner, which passed on the occult traditions of the HB of L and certain specific teachings that originated in another of our earlier honorees, African-American occultist Paschal Beverly Randolph. Reuss passed on the teachings of the OTO to Crowley and eventually made him the head of the OTO in the English-speaking world, setting off a cascade of events that would make a first-rate melodrama or a really good tell-all book. Since I'm not a member of the OTO, my only connection with any of that story is that one of my teachers was taught by a student of a student of Reuss...

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

comments.content:
“Those readers who have worked with the Greater Mysteries degrees of the Dolmen Arch will recognize some similarities between this material and the work they have learned. The two methods of healing are not identical but they're closely related and can be combined. Experimentation will show how to do this.”

I have avoided asking any questions about this combination due to this comment, but I have reached a point where I feel like I do need to get some input on the results of experimentation to determine how to best proceed with the MOE training. I interpreted “they’re not identical but can be combined” to mean that I could learn to do both as entirely separate practices and then later consider layering them in a healing session. As such, I’ve been mostly keeping MOE practice to early in the day and DA practice to late in the day.

For most of my time with the MOE material, I haven’t felt anything moving between the hands during the healing hands empowerment or when using the healing hands. In contrast, I do feel the energy moving when using the Dolmen Arch techniques quite strongly. I also feel the Healing Jewels heat up during empowerment, clearly feel the palm centers active throughout the day, and feel like empowering the palm centers in the morning increases my ability to sense and work with nwyfre in general and in particular makes work with the Dolmen Arch healing circuit even stronger. Judging by results, it also seems that in spite of not feeling it, I have successfully used the MOE healing technique to good effect.

The issue is that I’ve regularly gotten to the point where I feel like I can start to feel energy moving during the MOE healing technique, but it doesn’t feel like it’s exclusively coming out of the right palm and entering the left palm, rather like it’s coming out of both and kind of “wants” me to open the above and below connections of the DA technique. Basically, when I start to feel it, it feels like the DA technique, and I’ve kind of pushed this feeling to the side, assuming I’m making a mistake of not keeping the techniques separate, and tried to force it to feel like it’s only moving in the one way “circle” described in the MOE papers, at which point I feel nothing.

For one recent MOE healing, I decided to take a cue from what I understand to be John Gilbert’s approach, and totally relax and not “try” to feel it moving at all and simply ask the Divine to guide the flow. The above and below connections of the DA circuit immediately opened up, and the feeling during the healing work was quite notable. Further, I feel like the “foot centers” connecting to the Telluric Current opened even more strongly than they have before, to the point where they parallel the way the palm centers are empowered, connecting me even more strongly to the above and below DA version of the circuit. (This was after the Healer attunement, so I don’t know if there’s any kind of foot center awakening expected with that.)

So I’m starting to feel like I should just abandon the notion that “can be combined” means “kept isolated except at specific times when they’re overlaid” and simply let the techniques fully merge in my practice.

But I’ve wondered if perhaps I’m subconsciously retreating to the “familiar ground” of the DA technique that I already know, and if instead I should push through it and keep them isolated in order to properly learn the MOE technique.

What do you think?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The techniques can certainly be merged. I like to encourage people to learn them separately, as you've done, but once you've got both techniques down it's wholly acceptable to combine them, or simply to let them flow together. John used to say that every Essene healer gradually develops a unique personal way of healing, and merging the Essene method with other methods was often part of that. The important thing is to heal, not to heal in this or that way!

Volume 2: The Greater Mysteries

comments.title: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I have a question about wedding / engagement rings. My apologies if this not in your area of knowledge. I have a ring from my parents wedding, which was rather tumultuous and chaotic, including infidelity. I am interested in a couple options to use the ring as an engagement ring for my partner that would probably become part of her wedding band. I am on good terms with both of my parents if that matters in this process. I am looking at 4 possibilities for the ring.

1) Cleaning with vinegar and then alcohol and using it as is.
2) Having it modified by a goldsmith with some other either lab-made or antique gems (maybe aquamarine, her birthstone) and maybe some additional gold as needed and then giving it to her.
3) Have the goldsmith remove the diamonds, and melt down the gold to remake a new ring with a new design and perhaps use those same diamonds and/or any other good gems I can find.

4) Have the ring melted down and donate the money to charity (ie start completely fresh). Buy or have a ring made that has lab-made gems or an antique one that feels right.

My questions are -

1) Is there anything in these processes (the cleaning, the modifying, the melting down) that might cleanse any of the what I'm going to call 'negative energy' this ring may have absorbed from my parents' marriage?

2) Is this ring a bad idea in general? Does it not matter since I am a result of my parents marriage?

3) Anything special I should do with any gems I come across if they are to be used in a ring?


My partner has expressed an interest in not having freshly mined gems, etc because of the working conditions surrounding them. I imagine whatever gems I add should be found on one of my/ our hikes ie probably non-precious at best or should come from a good situation. I have no occult training so can't perform workings, and am not a member of any church or religious organization that could bless the ring.

If there is a body of knowledge or books you know of that you'd rather refer me to that would also be much appreciated. The information out there I've found so far looks heavily clouded with marketing.

replies.title: Re: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
replies.content: I would very strongly encourage you not to use that ring at all. An antique ring or newly made one with better energy, preferably one that doesn't include diamonds -- those correspond to Mars and tend to produce quarrels -- would be fine. Aquamarine would be a very good stone to use -- it's a variety of beryl, like emerald, and has similar peaceful and harmonious energies.

replies.title: Re: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
replies.content:
Thanks so much for the advice. If I sell the ring can I use that money to help with the costs of the ring or wedding ceremony? Or should I not use it on the wedding but can use it on mundane things like Princess Cutekitten suggests? Or is it best to give the ring back or donate it?

My parent gave it to me hoping it would help with the wedding-related costs, but if even using the money from it brings the karma / energy from their marriage into my new household and I can't change that, I'd prefer not to.

Luckily I found out there are a couple sources of aquamarine and other beryls in my state and have now found out that people still go rock-hunting in the old mines and near them and there is a place that cuts and polishes these stones, so I can get some aquamarine that hasn't been mined with child or slave labor or lots of nasty environmental consequences.

Thanks again!

replies.title: Re: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
replies.content: You can certainly sell the ring if you like, though I'd recommend washing it three times in cold water with a little salt in it, and then leaving it out in the sun for a while, so the purchaser is spared any lingering energies. (You're more vulnerable to those because of the blood connection with your parents.) Delighted to hear about the source for aquamarine -- something like that would guarantee good clean energies.

replies.title: Re: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
replies.content:
JMG, could you elaborate a bit more on why this would be a bad idea. I am in a similar situation and was planning to ask a jeweller to melt the gold and make new rings out of them. I was thinking of this as alchemy, i.e. turning my memories of a bad wedding from my parent into a happy one.

Is it the memories associated with it? Or is it something intrinsic to the gold now? How would that be different if the gold were sold and someone else wore the rings?

replies.title: Re: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
replies.content:
It depends entirely on how willing you are to deal with the energies of the earlier marriage. Magically speaking, those are part of the gold now, and you'll experience them. You can overcome them, but you'll need to make an effort to do that.

It's a problem for you because there's a blood link. Another person wouldn't have that -- and if you sold the gold, it would be melted down with a lot of other gold, and any ring made from it would have only a very small amount of that influence in it.

replies.title: Re: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
replies.content:
Don’t use vinegar! It could wreck the finish.

Another possibility is to skip the ring altogether and put the money towards house improvements, the possible appearance of a baby, charity, or whatever. My father had a ring but didn’t wear it for decades, as he worked with machinery. He was going to resume wearing it when he retired but by then they couldn’t find it. He didn’t recall pawning it when they were young and poor, so they figured it went walkabout during one of their moves. The lack of a ring did not keep the marriage from lasting 67 years, 364 days. (He died the day before the 68th anniversary.). So don’t be embarrassed to do without. Love and loyalty reside in the soul, not on a finger.

Also, congratulations, and I wish the two of you a happy 68 years!

Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Wedding / Engagement Ring Re-use
replies.content: Ok thanks for your thoughts!  And saving the ring from vinegar.  I've decided to go with aquamarine from a local source and see if I can get a jeweler to make a ring with that so it will hopefully be something with a lot more of my effort involved getting it made and maybe a bit less money which is what I would prefer to make it more special.  At a minimum more of the money will be staying local or regional than buying something from the chain stores.  If I could make the rings myself I would.

comments.title: An Oddball Question
comments.content:
I lost my glasses about 6 months ago. We took the house apart with no luck. I figured I had knocked them off my night table into the wastebasket, then obliviously emptied same the next day, especially since frequent appeals to St. Anthony did not lead either of us to the glasses. I kept putting off getting new ones in hopes of finding them. This was not a big problem as I’m old enough to be getting farsighted, though I do need them to read grocery-aisle signs.

Now. The other day I was kneeling atop my bed dusting. My glasses fell onto, and bounced off, the back of my heel. The angle was such that they had to have fallen straight down. If you’re wondering how I knew it was my glasses, I can only assure you that nothing in this world feels like a pair of glasses falling from the ceiling and bouncing off your heel.

Any idea how this could happen?

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: The traditional explanation was that such things were taken by the fairies. Having had things like that happen to me, too, I tend to think there's something to that particular tradition!

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content:
Is more to this tradition? I've had similar things happen that prevented regular activities from occurring and then, weeks later, the item would return in a place I could not have missed. It's happened several times throughout my life.

I got the impression that disrupting the routine was the point. Though I wasn't sure who the beneficiary of that interruption was, there was a sense that some calamity was averted.

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content:
Have you had things fall out of the air? Did you see it? Unfortunately I was facing the other way, but the angle of the bounce told me they fell straight down. I wish I had seen it.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: No matter which way you faced, it would have fallen behind you. They're clever that way. In my case, it's usually sitting on a table rather than dropped from someplace, but Sara and I do our best to maintain good relations with them.

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: Do you have any advice on how to maintain good relations with faeries?

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: We had a friend stay with us for a few weeks. We gave her a house key, which she said she left on the corner of the dining room table. We looked for days and couldn't fine it. A few weeks later I was standing by the table and heard a 'clink'. Looking down, there was the key. There was also a cat standing there but for the life of me I don't know where they would have found it...

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content:
Such things happen quite often here: sometimes they are returned - found in a most unlikely or even impossible place - and sometimes they just vanish forever.

There's usually a disinct element of humourous mischief in it (different to the unpleasant and malevolent haunting of 20 years ago) to which I now respond:

'Oh, that was a good one, very funny! Really, a master-stroke! But may I, please, have it back?'

I consider it's best to share the joke. There's certainly an element of intelligence in it, not merely random disappearances.

To my delight it occurred recently, while I was in the room, to a highly scientific friend, a Professor of Medicine at Cambridge University with zero interest in the occult, and left him open-mouthed but unable find any rational explanation other than an invisible, joking 'house spirit'.

The dog sometimes seems to watch something invisible to me, but never shows any sign of fear.

I reason that a house with a pleasant joker in residence, or even just passing through now and then, can't be a terribly bad place to live.

'More things in Heaven and Earth....' etc!

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content:
Sounds like those fairies are pretty busy!

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: How do the fairies that play such jokes around the house differ from the house spirits/gods like the Scandinavian Tomten or the Roman Lares? Are they the same?

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content:
I've had this happen before a few times, my real question in the deep mysteries of life...

Is there a sock goblin? Lol I have no explaination for the slow disappearance of the families sock supply, they just gradually diminish and I have to top up every few months. I'm sure there's a logical explaination, I've dug around my dryer etc, to no avail.

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: Some washers will wash socks right over the edge of the drum and down into the sewers, or so I've read. But yes, socks are the kinds of things that might attract the attention of fairies, being small and often interestingly colored.

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: Socks are the larval form of wire coat hangars.

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content: a practical suggestion: try pinning pairs of socks together with a large safety pin.

replies.title: Re: An Oddball Question
replies.content:
I once lost a banana in a 150 square foot dorm room. 4 people searched for over an hour and finally found it between the couch/bed cushions. I can only blame my own talent for losing things and not the fairies on that one.

That said, many years later in 2016 when my husband and I bought our current little house, I started putting out bird feeders and water for miscellaneous wandering animals. The first winter there was snowy and blustery. We were storing birdseed on the rickety old front porch and I had the S hook for one of the feeders in my hand. I was on the steps when the S hook slipped and fell into a crack between two of the steps. I knew there was no freaking way I was retrieving the hook until spring, so I made do with some hairbands and heaven knows what else and put the filled bird feeders back up that way. I did not tell anyone about the missing S hook or where it was. I just chalked it up to life being annoying. The next morning, I got in my car to drive to work. I felt something on my behind. The S hook was on my car seat. The car had been locked the whole night! I am convinced it was the fairies.

comments.content:
Dear JMG,
If you've been following the events in Ukraine at all, you might have seen coverage of the increasing use of pagan rune-based symbols on the Ukrainian side. This isn't new: the extreme nationalist/neo-nazi movements in Ukraine have been using them for years, but now they seem to pop up everywhere on uniforms and even at official functions.
These symbols were, of course, extensively used by the Nazi Waffen SS as unit symbols. comments.title: The Conjuration of the Four versus the Calling of the Elements
comments.content:
JMG,

As always, thank you for this Q&A.


By chance I opened Levi's Doctrine and Ritual to the ritual section and started reading Chapter 4, The Conjuration of the Four. In this chapter Levi mentions domination of the spirits and uses phrases such as "one must impose the Verb of one's will upon the elements". One exorcises the elements in this practice. Levi also stresses how the elementary spirits "most often torment those who deal with them".

This is in comparison with the Golden Section's calling of the elements for the Sphere of Protection and what is basically a request for their help: "May the powers of XXXX bless and protect me.....".

Is there a difference between the Golden Section and Levi's view of how to interact with and the nature of, the elements?

After thinking over this and additional reading I believe Levi's view on the nature of the elements is similar with the Golden Section incorporating much of Levi's thought (e.g. "They indifferently reproduce good and evil, because they have no free will..." and "The mage who employs their cooperation takes upon himself a terrible responsibility..."). replies.title: Re: The Conjuration of the Four versus the Calling of the Elements

My background includes Druid and Rosicrucian initiations and teachings, and I have always found the elements and their spirits to be cooperative if approached in a respectful and friendly manner; this was also the way John Gilbert taught his students to work. comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

This question is open to you and others.

What is your view, and the view of the occult or alternative spiritual traditions on debt, and particularly interest based debt?

I ask cos I’ve been fairly embedded in the world of finance the last decade and and am starting a banking and credit business, and thus the moral nature of what I’m about to embark on has been heavily on my mind lately.

While I am not from the Abrahamic tradition, all 3 of them seem to heavily prohibit interest rate based lending as a type of corruption. This proscription have been severely watered down via both legalistic interpretations (as among the Jews) as well as the general reformation and dilution (only high interest rates, rather than all interest rates as was previously considered) of the scripture that happened in the renaissance as banking took off and was closely linked to the church (incl the medicis installing one of their own).

In the world we are in now, this financial structure of debt based currencies, trade, finance and payments seems impossible to unseat without massive collateral damage.

Also, it’s hard to dispute that time value of money ( measured by interest or discount rates) has been a very useful capital allocation mechanism to finance all types of entrepreneurship and science, which was very difficult before when it was only accessible to elites.

Yet, the argument that interest rate based structures may have pernicious effects on the human mind, spirit and society weighs on my mind. I’m spending time thinking of how I can try to do things differently (there are many interesting attempts on-going at non-commodity money or local credit networks/ledgers, mutual credit obligations to trade goods and services directly etc but nothing that has truly cracked it and can be a viable alternative to the world of finance we’re subject to).


Any thoughts are welcome.


PS. I cast a I Ching asking “what is the moral nature of interest rates” and it returned Unchanging 18 (corruption). Hah, funny.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Are you sure it's not simply saying that interest rates are a Saturnine phenomenon? Because interest rates, decay, and hard work are all very Saturnine phenomena. "Corruption" in your sense seems more like 11 (stagnation). Decay includes positive things like "soil", "bioremediation", and "zymurgy".

You should be able to get most of the Saturn associations from comparing English translations. One you might miss is that in Chinese each of the line readings is the same short length (four characters).

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: I've never seen any discussion of the morality of lending at interest in occult literature. My own take is complex, and sees that as an inevitable evil that emerges over the course of a civilization's life cycle and plays an important role in bringing the civilization down. I don't think there's much likelihood that lending at interest can be replaced by some other mode of allocating collective capital, for the simple reason that the wealth and power of the ruling classes of our society depend on their control of capital and they can therefore be expected to fight like rabid honey badgers to preserve the system that maintains their power.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Interest rates are just how a private property market economy navigates certain naturally occurring tradeoffs, wherein you make a sacrifice of options that are slightly below your personally-market-clearing rate of return in order to free up resources for options that are slightly above your personally-market-clearing rate of return.

If you have to overcome interest-based finance, you might need a system of practice with a way of organizing and incentivizing a community's productive labor and capital allocation over time, that demonstrates itself to be better at benefiting from that kind of natural wealth-producing option, in a virtuous and balanced way, than the financial system is.

To give some idea of what this could look like: In an imaginary universe, where you had people who really understood on a visceral level how tradeoffs over time worked, and where they all had a consistent idea of how much they all cared about themselves and their beneficiaries now and in the future, you could get a situation where those people were naturally as productive as if they'd been lending and borrowing at interest, without any lending or borrowing at interest. What would normally have been a default on a loan in our universe would then, in that imaginary universe, just be an understandable failure from someone who was genuinely trying the-right-amount-of-hard. Not just in how hard they were working, but also in how carefully they were thinking through possibilities and realistically planning around long shots and misfortunes, and, in the long run, in how attentively they adjusted their habits of paying attention to some aspects of the world and not others, given the effects on what wealth-affecting possible outcomes they noticed. But then, all of that is like saying "in an imaginary universe where Communism worked". In our universe, if you tried to arrange people that way without either market discipline or a lot of non-obvious cultural technology, the enterprise would fail on many levels of kybernesis simultaneously. The usual economic terms are "free-rider problem" and "principal-agent problem", and there's also the sociobiology/ev-psych "coefficients of relatedness" to consider.

There's a little bit of social science about why a few communes succeed and most fail. Supposedly there was a finding where religious kibbutzim with more hours collectively spent in communal religious ritual time per week were more likely to succeed than secular kibbutzim. But I didn't find a citation. I did find that papers liked to bring up the question of what it does to a kibbutz or other kind of commune to be in implicit competition with an external market economy. That literature might be a good place to start reading, to understand what you'd have to get right.

comments.title: Geomancy and Water
comments.content:
Good Morning JMG and Ecosophia Commentariat,
Thank you for this space.

Borrowing a technique I enjoy from fellow commenter JPRussell:
To share:
If you are struggling with a daily Sphere of Protection (or likely any daily ritual) because you are over-busy, I empathize with you; and, I've found a couple tools that are helpful.

First, don't be afraid to play with time. I initially did every SOP slowly and reverently. I believe it is helpful to do the practice slowly as you are memorizing and developing it, but afterward I've found it far better to do a zippy SOP than to do none at all. Surprisingly, I've found that I've experienced some powerful visualizations and bodily effects while doing it quickly, I think because it pulled me out of my head and into other spaces.

Second, don't be afraid to play with time. I tried a couple times, while not in a hurry, to look at the clock and do the SOP with the intention that it is ritual time and outside of normal time, and then checked the clock at the end. I've found in doing so that my experience of time in-ritual is truly distinct from clock-time. This goes both ways though, so play with the concept at your own discretion, while not in a hurry. ;)

To ask:
1. Please explain the difference between Earth Divination Earth Magic and The Art and Practice of Geomancy. I have the former and am wondering if the later would be helpful as well or if they are different editions of the same material.

2. I've been pondering water... It is interesting that when cold it better cleanses your etheric body, and when warm it better cleanses physical bodies. Water has multiple physical properties that differ from other physical substances. We are mostly made of water. ... Perhaps water is a transfer or binding medium between the physical and etheric...? It's also interesting to go down the train of thought around hot/cold - the cold winter is often a time of inner work, the hot summer a time for physical work, and the in-between times of fall and spring are liminal spaces of fruitfulness and harvest. Perhaps these correspond to cycles of primacy of the non-physical (winter) and the physical (summer) bodies and then the life-giving exchange between them (fall/spring.) This seems to tie in with various pagan calendars. Is any of this discussed in any occult works you know of? I'm figuring that my aha-moment is expounded upon elsewhere.

3. I've found a couple interesting links to discursive meditation. One is the phenomena (and childhood game) of saying a word so much that it loses all meaning. Sometimes a meditation topic nearly dissolves upon repeated inspection, and it can be quite humorous to repeated think on something till it is nonsense. The other is one of my favorite quotes by Werner Heisenberg: “The first gulp from the glass of natural sciences will turn you into an atheist, but at the bottom of the glass God is waiting for you.” The first moments of thinking on a subject make me feel powerful, whereas the final thoughts on the subject are often humbling as I realize “as the island of Knowledge grows, so do the shores of our ignorance" (Marcelo Gleiser)
Do you (or others) have a similar experience?

Apologies for the profusion of thoughts, and perhaps a dearth of clear questions. I very much appreciate this space to share and receive.

May you all be blessed (if you'd like to be.)

comments.title: MM Queries

Hi,

It has been a while since my last visit to MM, so here goes.

JMG, I know you have done return and transit charts against natal for your own life, so your experience here is valuable:

1a. If you have a solar return chart, and the lord of the ascendant is retrograde, does this mean the significations of that planet as lord of the year are weakened?

replies.title: Re: The path of the hearth-fire
replies.content: The Way of the Hearth-Fire, as Dion Fortune calls it, is a way of spiritual training suitable for those whose commitments don't give them the time or the freedom to devote many hours a day to magical and spiritual work. It consists of treating the routine of daily life as a magical practice: doing everything with intention, keeping spiritual values in mind in all things, and acting accordingly. She discusses it in detail in her book The Training and Work of the Initiate.

comments.content:
I'd like to hear your thoughts about the potential sources of J. R. R. Tolkien's creative works. Many people feel there are deeper spiritual realities reflected in it, and yet, if there were, I think there would be more evidence of it than Tolkien's work. There are no temples dedicated to the Valar, and the suggestion that we just call them by different names doesn't have a lot of weight. And yet, the Lord of the Rings has at times outsold the bible.

Despite this, there's something uniquely stirring and nourishing to my soul in his work. It seems that he was in touch with a very potent source. And maybe not the only one. Dr Becca Tarnas has written about the fascinating synchronicities between Jung and Tolkien, both writing their own Red Books around the same time, pointing towards a shared catalyst for their experiences.

Tolkien's style of writing sounds more like journeying or scrying, and exploring places and stories that already had their own existence, which sounds similar to the way you've described your own writing process.

Why do you think Tolkien's work has so much power above and beyond most if not all other fantasy stories? Given the prominent place of his version of fairy lore in his writing and the way some of his stories are presented as accounts of entering the otherworld, do you think he may have made contact with the fae or been guided in his storytelling by them or some other beings?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's an immense question and I have no easy answers to it. I've long suspected, however, that Tolkien -- devout and conservative Roman Catholic though he was in later life -- dabbled, or more than dabbled, in Christian occultism in his youth. There's just too much Theosophy in his fiction: "I am a servant of the Secret Fire, wielder of the flame of Anor" is one example. What is the Secret Fire? Any Theosophist could have told you. Then there's his Atlantis myth, which is heavily influenced by Blavatsky's account in The Secret Doctrine.

BTW, Tarnas isn't the only one to notice that. Timothy O'Neill's book The Individuated Hobbit, published in 1979, covered the parallels between Tolkien and Jung in quite some detail.

comments.title: Absolute Numerical Value of Aleph
comments.content:
Question for anyone familiar with Gematria numerology (Hebrew numerology): what do you consider the absolute numerical value of aleph (א) to be?

- I thought the absolute value would be 1 as it is the first letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

- Later on, thoughts came it may be 2 as a representation of "as above / so below" or even an absolute value of 3 "as above / so below and the adept depicted in the magician tarot card.

- I just came across this "The numerical value of Aleph is 111 (Aleph + Lamed + Peh: 1 + 30 + 80 = 111). The number 111 contains the trinity; and it is also the constant of the magic square of six. 111 = 1 + 10 +100."

Context for the question is a riddle Eliphas Levi left in his book, The Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic (CH 21 pg. 178: "He who understands perfectly the absolute numerical value of א multiplied by N, with the grammatical force of the final N in the words.......")

comments.title: Saturn Opposition
comments.content:
Saturn is just moving into Pisces and another cohort of people in their forties will be getting their second Saturn Opposition. I am aware of the general idea of working with Saturn, but I am not sure what exactly is meant, and how individual it is. The period of my first Saturn Opposition, by which I mean the period the planet was in the sign, was very successful in important respects except for a major issue that started right at the end of it, quite far away from the exact degree of opposition. My Saturn return was very difficult and dramatic. If it is possible to see one unified message in both of these periods, what led to success in the first opposition, and what I was pushed towards by the return, it is that Saturn likes me in what youngsters these days call "monk mode". This currently, usually looks more possible for me than it does for the average middle aged person, for example, I do not have children. But I am wondering how far I should go in actively trying to recreate the aspects that were helpful the first time - but not those that were bad for me. I appear rationalist to most people I know in every day life, so to use astrology as the deciding factor for any important decision I am unsure about feels like a little too much.

I am also wondering about Saturn oppositions and house moves, as I would be surprised if I were still living in the same property by the time Saturn goes into Aries. It is hardly unusual for people in their mid forties to move home, and this will be fine for plenty of them. We did not move, or try to, when I was in my first Saturn Opposition. Is it a good idea to use some extra prayers or offerings if this comes up? I would normally do planetary charity and candle burning anyway.

replies.title: Re: Saturn Opposition
replies.content: All this depends on what else in your natal chart transiting Saturn is aspecting, and what else is transiting at the same time. That's why no two Saturn returns or Saturn oppositions are the same -- by the time you get to the second one, the planetary positions you experienced in the first one have changed completely. I recommend a thorough delineation of your progressed chart or charts for the period of the opposition -- that will give you enough to go on.
